summary: The largest former-Yugoslav state, an EU candidate that still leans on Russia and China, refuses to recognise Kosovo, and plays a careful balancing act between East and West.

history.detail: Defeat by the Ottomans becomes the central myth of Serbian nationhood.
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo sparks world war.
Wars erupt as Slovenia, Croatia and others secede.
NATO bombs Serbia over Kosovo; Milošević withdraws forces.
Popular uprising ends his rule; he is later tried at The Hague.
Serbia rejects it and still claims the territory.

key_doctrines: Balancing between Russia/China and the EU

finance.currency_role: Serbian dinar (RSD); managed float, EU accession pending

tech_startups.vc_ecosystem: Growing IT outsourcing and gaming sector

resources.oil_gas: Modest; dependent on Russian gas via pipelines
resources.nuclear: None; constitutional ban on nuclear power
resources.rare_earths: Large Jadar lithium deposit (Rio Tinto project, contested)
resources.water: Adequate; Danube and Sava river basins
resources.agriculture: Strong; maize, wheat, fruit; a regional food exporter

follow_the_money.detail: Smederevo steel and Bor copper mines bought by Chinese firms
Gazprom owns oil firm NIS; Serbia relies on Russian gas
Europe's largest lithium project, repeatedly stalled by protests

theories_disputed.detail: International courts ruled the 1995 massacre of 8,000 Bosniaks a genocide; some Serb officials still dispute the term.

connections.detail: Slavic-Orthodox ties; refuses to sanction Moscow
Strategic partner; major mining and infrastructure investor
Refuses recognition; periodic flashpoints in the north
